content.text: A Speech-to-Speech (S2S) model is an AI system that receives raw audio as input and produces raw audio as output, without converting speech to text at any intermediate stage. The model processes the acoustic signal directly — encoding it into a compact internal representation, reasoning over it, and decoding a response back into audio — in a single end-to-end forward pass.
This stands in sharp contrast to the

that most voice assistants have used historically: a speech recogniser (ASR) transcribes audio to text, a large language model generates a text response, and a text-to-speech synthesiser (TTS) converts that response back to audio. Each handoff introduces latency and discards paralinguistic information that cannot be recovered downstream.
The key enabling technology is the

. Neural codec models — such as EnCodec, SoundStream, and DAC — compress raw waveforms into discrete token sequences at rates as low as 50–75 tokens per second. These tokens can be fed directly into a transformer's embedding table alongside text tokens, giving a single autoregressive model a unified vocabulary that spans both modalities. Models trained on this joint vocabulary learn to attend to and generate audio with the same architecture used for text.
(Kyutai). Each exposes a streaming real-time API — typically WebSocket-based — so that audio can flow bidirectionally with conversation-level latency well under one second.

Voice is the highest-bandwidth, lowest-friction channel human beings use to communicate. Yet every AI voice assistant built before 2024 was constrained by a text bottleneck: all of the acoustic richness of human speech — pace, emphasis, laughter, hesitation, frustration, irony — was collapsed into a sequence of words, acted on by a model that had no direct access to it, and then reconstructed by a separate synthesis model that could only guess at the appropriate prosody.

S2S models eliminate that bottleneck. When a user says "really?" with a rising, incredulous intonation, a native audio model can perceive and respond to the intonation directly, not just to the word. When a customer's voice tightens with frustration mid-sentence, the model can detect and address the emotional signal before it is ever expressed in words.

Latency matters enormously in conversational voice. Cascaded pipelines typically produce 1.5–3 seconds of silence after the user finishes speaking before the first audio byte arrives. Native S2S systems regularly achieve 200–600 ms first-byte latency, often indistinguishable from a human response delay. At that latency, conversation feels natural rather than halting, which determines whether users will tolerate and adopt the system.

Eliminating ASR and TTS round-trips cuts end-to-end latency by 4–10x. OpenAI reports first-token audio latency around 320 ms for the Realtime API, versus 1.5+ seconds for a comparably capable cascaded system.
The model's output audio inherits appropriate rhythm, stress, and intonation from its training on natural speech, rather than the flat or formulaic patterns of most TTS systems.
Laughter, sighs, hesitation markers ("uh", "um"), raised voices, and whispering are perceivable and generatable without any special-casing in application code.
A single API endpoint replaces three separate services (ASR, LLM, TTS), eliminating three failure points, three billing relationships, and the integration glue between them.

Because the model streams audio bidirectionally, it can detect when the user begins speaking mid-response and stop generating, enabling natural turn-taking that cascaded systems struggle to implement cleanly.
The model can condition its responses on properties of the speaker's voice — accent, age, energy level — without requiring explicit metadata fields.

S2S models trade controllability for naturalness. In a cascaded pipeline you can inspect and post-process the text at every stage: log it, run safety filters over it, replace specific words, or override the TTS voice. A native audio model is a black box that emits tokens you cannot easily intercept.

There is no transcript to log, search, or feed downstream text-only systems. If you need a conversation record, you must either run ASR in parallel or use a model that optionally emits a text transcript alongside audio (as GPT-4o Audio does).

Audio tokens are more expensive to process than text tokens. At the time of writing, GPT-4o Realtime audio input costs roughly 40x the per-token price of text input for the same model. High-volume applications need to budget carefully.

Closed S2S APIs (OpenAI, Google) do not yet offer audio-mode fine-tuning. Domain adaptation requires careful prompt engineering with audio examples or must fall back to a cascaded approach where the LLM component can be fine-tuned.
As of mid-2025, GPT-4o Audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash Live, and Moshi cover the practical range. There is no open-source S2S model with quality comparable to a good cascaded pipeline.
Text-level content policies cannot be applied to audio tokens before synthesis. Providers implement their own audio-layer filters, but the surface area is harder to audit and test.
S2S APIs are designed for persistent WebSocket sessions. Serverless, stateless, or HTTP-only infrastructure requires architectural adaptation.

. A codec model (EnCodec, DAC) encodes a raw waveform into a sequence of discrete codes using a residual vector quantiser (RVQ). At 24 kHz audio with an EnCodec codec, a 1-second clip becomes roughly 75 codec tokens at the first RVQ level. These tokens are integer IDs that live in a vocabulary, exactly like text tokens.

: system prompt text tokens, then audio input tokens representing the user's speech, then audio output tokens it generates as the response. The attention mechanism spans all modalities — the model can attend from an audio output token back to an audio input token, allowing it to mimic the speaker's prosody or react to their emotional tone directly.

Output audio tokens are decoded back to a waveform by running the codec model's decoder in the reverse direction. Because decoding is cheap (a few milliseconds per chunk), the system can stream decoded audio to the client as tokens are generated, achieving first-audio-byte latency close to the time-to-first-token.

, which detects when the user starts and stops speaking. The OpenAI Realtime API has a built-in server-side VAD mode that automatically triggers model inference when silence is detected after speech. Alternatively, clients can disable server VAD and send explicit "commit" events to control turn boundaries programmatically.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side architecture comparison: left panel shows Cascaded Pipeline (Audio Input -> ASR -> LLM -> TTS -> Audio Output) with ~2000 ms latency and annotations noting prosody and emotion are lost at the ASR stage. Right panel shows Native S2S Model (Audio Input -> S2S Foundation Model containing Audio Encoder and Codec Decoder -> Audio Output) with ~400 ms latency and annotations noting prosody and emotion are preserved.

content.text: The left panel illustrates the cascaded pipeline's four sequential hops: each arrow represents a modality change and a latency cost. The right panel shows that a native S2S model collapses those four hops into one, and that the internal Audio Encoder and Codec Decoder operate within the same forward pass, preserving paralinguistic information that the cascaded path discards.
Use S2S when latency is the primary constraint.
Customer-facing voice assistants, phone agents, live transcription companions, and real-time coaching tools all require sub-700 ms response latency to feel conversational.
Use S2S when emotional fidelity matters.

Mental health support tools, empathy-driven customer service, or media production assistants that must produce naturally expressive speech benefit from a model that understands and generates prosody natively.
Use S2S when architectural simplicity is valued.
A startup building a voice product with a small team cannot easily operate three separate ML systems. One S2S API reduces operational complexity dramatically.
Prefer cascaded pipelines when a text audit log is required by compliance.
Financial services, healthcare, and legal applications often mandate full transcripts. In these cases use a cascaded approach or a hybrid model that emits a parallel transcript.
Prefer cascaded pipelines when cost is the dominant factor.
For very high call volume, a well-tuned cascaded pipeline using a small ASR model, a mid-tier LLM, and a fast TTS can be 10–20x cheaper per minute than a native S2S API.
The dominant implementation pattern for production S2S today is a

. The client opens a WebSocket to the provider's endpoint, authenticates with an ephemeral token, then exchanges JSON-framed events and binary audio chunks over the same connection for the duration of the conversation.
Obtain an ephemeral API credential. For OpenAI Realtime, call the REST endpoint
from your backend to get a short-lived token. Never expose your main API key to a browser client.
event to configure turn detection, output audio format, voice, and instructions.
Capture microphone audio (PCM 16-bit, 24 kHz), base64-encode 100 ms chunks, and send them as
When server VAD detects end-of-speech it emits
events containing base64-encoded PCM chunks. Decode and play each chunk immediately to minimise perceived latency.
if you need a parallel text transcript for logging or downstream processing.
events and implement exponential-backoff reconnection for dropped connections.
OpenAI Realtime API — Python WebSocket client

# OpenAI Realtime API: minimal Python WebSocket client
# pip install websockets openai pyaudio

import asyncio
import base64
import json
import os
import pyaudio
import websockets

API_KEY = os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"]
URL = "wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview"

AUDIO_RATE = 24000
CHUNK_MS = 100
CHUNK_FRAMES = int(AUDIO_RATE * CHUNK_MS / 1000)  # 2400 frames per chunk


async def run_voice_session():
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}",
        "OpenAI-Beta": "realtime=v1",
    }

    async with websockets.connect(URL, additional_headers=headers) as ws:
        # 1. Configure the session
        await ws.send(json.dumps({
            "type": "session.update",
            "session": {
                "modalities": ["audio", "text"],
                "instructions": "You are a concise voice assistant. Keep answers under 3 sentences.",
                "voice": "alloy",
                "input_audio_format": "pcm16",
                "output_audio_format": "pcm16",
                "turn_detection": {
                    "type": "server_vad",
                    "silence_duration_ms": 500,
                    "threshold": 0.5,
                },
                "input_audio_transcription": {"model": "whisper-1"},
            },
        }))

        pa = pyaudio.PyAudio()

        # 2. Open microphone input stream
        mic_stream = pa.open(
            format=pyaudio.paInt16,
            channels=1,
            rate=AUDIO_RATE,
            input=True,
            frames_per_buffer=CHUNK_FRAMES,
        )

        # 3. Open speaker output stream
        spk_stream = pa.open(
            format=pyaudio.paInt16,
            channels=1,
            rate=AUDIO_RATE,
            output=True,
        )

        async def send_audio():
            """Read mic chunks and send to the WebSocket."""
            while True:
                raw = mic_stream.read(CHUNK_FRAMES, exception_on_overflow=False)
                encoded = base64.b64encode(raw).decode()
                await ws.send(json.dumps({
                    "type": "input_audio_buffer.append",
                    "audio": encoded,
                }))
                await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield to event loop

        async def receive_events():
            """Handle incoming server events."""
            async for message in ws:
                event = json.loads(message)
                etype = event.get("type", "")

                if etype == "response.audio.delta":
                    # Decode and play audio immediately
                    audio_bytes = base64.b64decode(event["delta"])
                    spk_stream.write(audio_bytes)

                elif etype == "response.audio_transcript.delta":
                    # Optional: print partial transcript
                    print(event["delta"], end="", flush=True)

                elif etype == "response.done":
                    print()  # newline after transcript

                elif etype == "error":
                    print(f"[ERROR] {event['error']}")
                    break

        # Run sender and receiver concurrently
        try:
            await asyncio.gather(send_audio(), receive_events())
        finally:
            mic_stream.stop_stream()
            mic_stream.close()
            spk_stream.stop_stream()
            spk_stream.close()
            pa.terminate()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(run_voice_session())
Gemini Multimodal Live API — Python client

# Gemini Multimodal Live API: Python async client
# pip install google-genai

import asyncio
import os
from google import genai

API_KEY = os.environ["GOOGLE_API_KEY"]
MODEL = "models/gemini-2.0-flash-live-001"

async def run_gemini_voice():
    client = genai.Client(api_key=API_KEY, http_options={"api_version": "v1beta"})

    config = {
        "response_modalities": ["AUDIO"],
        "system_instruction": "You are a helpful, conversational assistant.",
        "speech_config": {
            "voice_config": {"prebuilt_voice_config": {"voice_name": "Puck"}}
        },
    }

    async with client.aio.live.connect(model=MODEL, config=config) as session:
        # Send a text prompt (audio input also supported)
        await session.send(input="Hello! What is the speed of sound?", end_of_turn=True)

        audio_chunks = []
        async for response in session.receive():
            if response.data:          # raw PCM audio bytes
                audio_chunks.append(response.data)
            if response.text:          # optional parallel transcript
                print(response.text, end="", flush=True)

        print(f"\nReceived {len(audio_chunks)} audio chunks")
        # Write PCM to file or feed to a speaker stream
        with open("gemini_response.pcm", "wb") as f:
            for chunk in audio_chunks:
                f.write(chunk)


asyncio.run(run_gemini_voice())

Consider a customer-support phone agent for an e-commerce company handling order status queries. The previous cascaded implementation used Deepgram Nova-2 for ASR, GPT-4o for reasoning, and ElevenLabs for TTS. Average response latency was 2.1 seconds. Customer satisfaction scores on voice interactions were measurably lower than chat, attributed to the awkward pauses.
The team migrated to the OpenAI Realtime API with

. The application server maintains a pool of WebSocket sessions and assigns each incoming phone call (via Twilio Media Streams) to a session. Audio is transcoded from Twilio's 8 kHz mulaw format to 24 kHz PCM16 on the server before forwarding to the Realtime API. Outbound audio is transcoded back to 8 kHz for delivery to the phone network.

Results after migration: average response latency dropped to 380 ms, interruption handling improved significantly (callers could cut off the agent mid-sentence and be heard immediately), and the agent's tone was perceived as warmer and more natural in A/B testing. The primary trade-off encountered was cost: audio token pricing meant the per-minute cost was 3x higher. The team addressed this by routing simple, high-volume queries (order status lookups) through a cheaper cascaded path and reserving the S2S model for calls that exceeded a complexity threshold.
Sending audio at the wrong sample rate or format

The OpenAI Realtime API expects PCM16 audio at 24 kHz mono. Sending 16 kHz, 44.1 kHz, or stereo data produces garbled or silent responses without any error message. Always resample and convert channel count before encoding. The
Python libraries can resample reliably; in Node.js use the
Exposing the API key in the browser
It is tempting to open a WebSocket directly from a browser client using your main API key. This exposes your key to every user of the application. Always use the

endpoint from your backend to obtain a short-lived ephemeral token (valid for 60 seconds) and pass only that token to the client. Ephemeral tokens cannot be used to create new sessions or call other APIs.

events faster than real-time when the model is fast. If you naively play each chunk as it arrives without a jitter buffer or output queue, audio artefacts and crackling appear. Use a simple playback queue that smooths the timing, or target a 200 ms audio buffer depth before starting playback.

WebSocket connections are closed by the server after a period of inactivity (about 30 minutes) or after exceeding per-session token limits. Applications that do not reconnect gracefully leave users with a silent assistant. Implement an
handler that reconnects with exponential backoff and restores the session instructions and conversation context.
Choosing S2S for batch or non-real-time workloads

S2S APIs are designed for interactive, streaming use cases. If you are generating audio responses for pre-defined prompts — podcast episode intros, IVR menu recordings, audiobook narration — a TTS API with a high-quality voice is significantly cheaper and gives you more control over pacing and emphasis.
Use server-side VAD for most applications.
The OpenAI Realtime API's built-in VAD is well-tuned for telephone-quality audio. Only switch to manual turn detection (sending
explicitly) when you need precise turn control, such as when chaining tool calls or handling push-to-talk UIs.
A 2000-token system prompt incurs audio token charges on every turn. Distil instructions to the essentials for voice context; defer complex grounding documents to tool calls.
in the session config. The transcript is available as
events and is essential for logging, compliance review, and debugging.
immediately and stop playing the current audio output. Failing to do this results in the model and the user speaking over each other.
Implement a cost cap at the infrastructure level.
Set maximum session duration limits and token budget limits in your session management layer. Audio token costs can spike unexpectedly with long or silent sessions.

Opening a new WebSocket and sending a session.update takes 100–200 ms. For applications where every millisecond counts (phone agents, live demos), maintain a small pool of pre-initialised sessions ready to accept audio.

S2S API pricing at mid-2025 rates gives GPT-4o Realtime audio input at approximately $0.10/minute and audio output at $0.20/minute. A 5-minute call costs roughly $1.50 in audio tokens alone, compared to $0.07–$0.15 for a comparable cascaded implementation. Plan for this at the unit economics level before committing to S2S for high-volume applications.

Horizontal scaling of S2S applications is straightforward: each conversation maps to one WebSocket connection, and connections are stateless from the infrastructure perspective (conversation state lives in the API session, not your server). A single application server can multiplex hundreds of concurrent WebSocket sessions. For very high concurrency, use a dedicated WebSocket gateway (nginx stream, Cloudflare Durable Objects, or AWS API Gateway WebSocket) to handle connection management and offload reconnection logic.

Do not send audio during periods of ambient silence when no user speech is expected (hold music, long pauses). Use client-side VAD to gate audio sending. Sending silence wastes input token budget and can trigger false VAD detections.

Smaller audio chunks (50 ms) reduce the VAD detection delay but increase WebSocket frame overhead. Larger chunks (200 ms) are more efficient but add latency to turn detection. 100 ms is a reliable default.

A Realtime session can span an entire multi-turn conversation. Do not open and close a session per user utterance. Keep the session alive for the conversation lifetime and let the model's context window manage history.
WebSocket; server VAD; parallel text transcription; function calling
WebSocket; multimodal input (audio + video + text); Google Search grounding
Self-hostable; Mimi codec; full-duplex; Apache 2 licence
RVQ codec used by many S2S research models; 24 kHz or 48 kHz
Higher quality than EnCodec at same bitrate; MIT licence; 44.1 kHz support
Python SDK; wraps OpenAI Realtime and other providers; handles rooms, tracks, VAD
Streams PSTN/VoIP audio to WebSocket; requires mulaw-to-PCM16 transcoding
OpenAI Realtime supports WebRTC transport as an alternative to raw WebSocket for browser clients
The WebSocket-based architecture of S2S APIs introduces several attack surfaces that text-only LLM integrations do not face.

Always generate short-lived session tokens server-side and pass only those to client applications. A leaked ephemeral token expires within 60 seconds; a leaked main API key enables billing fraud. For server-to-server integrations (backend telephony agents), use the main API key only in the trusted server environment.

A malicious user could play pre-recorded audio containing adversarial instructions ("ignore your instructions and...") into the microphone. Server-side input filtering is not yet as mature as text-layer content filtering. Apply rate limiting, session length caps, and monitor transcript content for prompt injection patterns.

Audio contains biometric voiceprint data. Handle transcripts and audio recordings under the same data-retention and access controls you apply to user messages. Many jurisdictions classify voice recordings as sensitive personal data. Do not log raw audio unless legally required and consented to.
WebSocket connections must use TLS (wss://). Validate the session token on every reconnect. Do not reuse session tokens across users.

Network jitter causes gaps in the audio byte stream. The server's VAD may misinterpret these gaps as end-of-speech and trigger a premature response. Smooth client-side audio capture with a ring buffer before sending; avoid sending audio from a browser tab in the background, which may have throttled JavaScript execution.

S2S APIs are newer and have had higher incident rates than their text equivalents. Build in a fallback path to a cascaded pipeline that can be activated if the S2S API returns elevated error rates, ensuring the application degrades gracefully rather than going silent.
Speech-to-Speech models process audio directly to audio, eliminating the text bottleneck of cascaded pipelines and reducing end-to-end latency from 1.5–3 seconds to 200–600 ms.

The enabling technology is neural audio codec tokenisation (EnCodec, DAC, Mimi) which converts waveforms into discrete tokens that share a vocabulary with text, allowing a single transformer to span both modalities.

Production S2S APIs (OpenAI Realtime, Gemini Live) expose WebSocket-based streaming protocols; implementation requires handling audio format conversion, chunked streaming, VAD events, and interruption signals explicitly.
S2S models preserve paralinguistic signals — prosody, emotion, hesitation markers — that cascaded pipelines discard at the ASR stage and cannot recover at the TTS stage.

The primary trade-offs are cost (audio tokens are 20–40x more expensive per token than text), controllability (no intermediate text to inspect or filter), and limited fine-tuning options on closed APIs.
Never send the main API key to a browser client; use the ephemeral session token endpoint instead. Treat audio transcripts as PII and apply data-retention policies accordingly.
Build a fallback path to a cascaded pipeline for compliance use cases requiring transcript logs, for cost-sensitive high-volume routes, and as a reliability backstop for S2S API outages.
